text: Ports are placed in the left upper quadrant in a straight line, spaced eight centimeters apart towards the right lower quadrant. The transverse colon is sutured to the abdominal wall to free robotic instruments for dissection and exposure. The right colon is also sutured to the abdominal wall for proper suspension and exposure.

The surgeon opens the mesenteric envelope and dissects the tumors using a medial-lateral approach. The duodenum is exposed, and the ileocolic vessels are identified. The ileocolic vein, branching from the superior mesenteric vein, is clipped and divided. The distal ileum is identified, and the mesentery is divided to address adhesions in the pelvis, freeing the small intestine for later anastomosis.

The surgeon uses the robotic fourth arm to continue cephalad dissection, identifying and clipping the right colic vein. The right colic artery is also identified, clipped, and divided using robotic clips, with no laparoscopic-assisted instruments utilized during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects over the pancreas and duodenum, demonstrating proximity to these structures. The suture is taken down for further retraction using the fourth arm, and the right branch of the midcolic vessels is identified, clipped, and divided.

The surgical team replaces a sponge underneath the transverse colon and pulls it caudally to enter the lesser sac. They divide the omentum and identify the right gastroepiploic vessels on the greater curvature of the stomach, which are then divided. The dissection continues towards the hepatic flexure, connecting spaces around the gastrocolic trunk, and mobilizing the right colon by releasing tension from a previously placed suture.

The surgeon divides the distal ileum and marks it with a suture, then divides the transverse colon using a vessel sealer. All remaining attachments are severed, and the specimen is placed aside. The distal ileum is positioned close to the transverse colon, and the anastomosis is prepared in the right upper quadrant with a suture, utilizing the fourth arm for exposure.

The surgeon performs an isoperistaltic anastomosis between the small intestine and transverse colon. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the blood supply to both structures. A 45 mm stapler is employed to create the anastomosis, and the fourth arm is used to align it. The common channel opening is closed using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a canal suture technique.

The completed anastomosis is shown with the barbed suture locked in place. Indocyanine green (ICG) is used to assess the blood supply to the anastomosis, confirming proper perfusion. The specimen is extracted through the stapler port, revealing the complete mesocolic excision with visible omentum and mesentery attached to the right and transverse colon. The specimen is identified as a well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor, with examination of 33 lymph nodes showing T3 N0 M0 staging.
